concepts.title: Introduction to Arrays
concepts.content_html:
Arrays in Java

An array is a fixed-size collection of elements of the same data type, stored in contiguous memory. Arrays let you store multiple values in a single variable.

Declaring and Initializing Arrays

// Declaration and initialization
int[] numbers = new int[5];          // 5 elements, all default to 0
String[] names = {"Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"}; // 3 elements
double[] grades = new double[]{95.5, 87.3, 92.1};

Accessing Elements

Array elements are accessed by their index, starting at 0:

int[] arr = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
System.out.println(arr[0]); // 10 (first element)
System.out.println(arr[4]); // 50 (last element)
arr[2] = 99;                // Change third element to 99

Array Length

Use .length (no parentheses!) to get the size of an array:

int[] data = {1, 2, 3, 4};
System.out.println(data.length); // 4

Traversing an Array

// Standard for loop
for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
    System.out.println(arr[i]);
}

// Enhanced for loop (for-each)
for (int value : arr) {
    System.out.println(value);
}
Key Takeaway: Arrays are zero-indexed (first element is at index 0) and fixed in size once created. Use .length to avoid going out of bounds.
Common Mistake: Accessing arr[arr.length] causes an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException! The last valid index is arr.length - 1.

problems.question_html:
What is the output of the following code?

int[] arr = {5, 10, 15, 20};
System.out.println(arr[2]);
problems.question_raw: What does arr[2] print when arr = {5, 10, 15, 20}?

hints.text: Arrays are zero-indexed. Index 0 is the first element.

solution_steps.text: Array indices: arr[0]=5, arr[1]=10, arr[2]=15, arr[3]=20.
arr[2] is the third element, which is 15.

problems.question_html:
What is the output of the following code?

int[] nums = new int[3];
System.out.println(nums[0]);
problems.question_raw: What prints when you access nums[0] of a new int[3]?

hints.text: When you create an array with new int[3], what are the default values for int?
In Java, numeric arrays are initialized to 0 by default.

solution_steps.text: When an int array is created with new int[3], all elements default to 0.

problems.question_html:
What happens when you run this code?

int[] arr = {1, 2, 3};
System.out.println(arr[3]);
problems.question_raw: What happens with arr[3] when arr has 3 elements?

hints.text: The array has indices 0, 1, and 2. What happens when you access index 3?
Accessing an index beyond arr.length - 1 causes an exception.

solution_steps.text: The array has 3 elements at indices 0, 1, 2.
Index 3 is out of bounds (valid range is 0 to arr.length-1 = 2).
Java throws an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException at runtime.

problems.question_html:
What is the output?

int[] arr = {3, 7, 1, 9, 4};
System.out.println(arr.length);
problems.question_raw: What does arr.length print for {3, 7, 1, 9, 4}?

hints.text: .length returns the number of elements in the array.
Count the elements: 3, 7, 1, 9, 4 = five elements.

problems.question_html:
What is the output?

int[] arr = {2, 4, 6, 8};
int sum = 0;
for (int val : arr) {
    sum += val;
}
System.out.println(sum);
problems.question_raw: What is the sum of {2, 4, 6, 8} using a for-each loop?

hints.text: The for-each loop visits each element: 2, 4, 6, 8. Add them all up.

solution_steps.text: The loop adds each element to sum: 0+2=2, 2+4=6, 6+6=12, 12+8=20.

problems.question_html: Which of the following correctly declares an array of 10 Strings in Java?
problems.question_raw: Which correctly declares an array of 10 Strings?

hints.text: The syntax is: Type[] name = new Type[size];
Square brackets go after the type (or variable name), and new Type[size] uses square brackets.

solution_steps.text: Java array declaration syntax: Type[] varName = new Type[size];
String[] names = new String[10]; is the correct syntax.
The other options have syntax errors (missing brackets, wrong placement, parentheses instead of brackets).

concepts.title: Array Algorithms
concepts.content_html:
Common Array Algorithms

These algorithms appear frequently on the AP CS A exam and in real programming. Master them!

Linear Search

Search through an array one element at a time:

public static int linearSearch(int[] arr, int target) {
    for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
        if (arr[i] == target) {
            return i; // Found at index i
        }
    }
    return -1; // Not found
}

Finding Maximum and Minimum

public static int findMax(int[] arr) {
    int max = arr[0];
    for (int i = 1; i < arr.length; i++) {
        if (arr[i] > max) {
            max = arr[i];
        }
    }
    return max;
}

Computing Sum and Average

int sum = 0;
for (int val : arr) {
    sum += val;
}
double average = (double) sum / arr.length;

Reversing an Array

for (int i = 0; i < arr.length / 2; i++) {
    int temp = arr[i];
    arr[i] = arr[arr.length - 1 - i];
    arr[arr.length - 1 - i] = temp;
}
Key Takeaway: These algorithms form the building blocks of more complex programs. Practice tracing through them by hand with sample arrays.
Pro Tip: When finding max/min, initialize with the first element of the array (not 0 or Integer.MAX_VALUE) to handle arrays with all negative numbers.

problems.question_html:
What does the following method return when called with linearSearch({5, 3, 8, 1, 9}, 8)?

public static int linearSearch(int[] arr, int target) {
    for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
        if (arr[i] == target) return i;
    }
    return -1;
}
problems.question_raw: What does linearSearch({5,3,8,1,9}, 8) return?

hints.text: The method returns the index where the target is found.
Walk through: i=0 (5!=8), i=1 (3!=8), i=2 (8==8) — return 2.

problems.question_html:
What is the output?

int[] arr = {4, 7, 2, 9, 1};
int max = arr[0];
for (int i = 1; i < arr.length; i++) {
    if (arr[i] > max) {
        max = arr[i];
    }
}
System.out.println(max);
problems.question_raw: What is the max of {4, 7, 2, 9, 1}?

hints.text: Trace through: max starts at 4, then gets updated whenever a larger value is found.
max=4, then 7>4 so max=7, 2<7, 9>7 so max=9, 1<9.

problems.question_html:
What is the output?

int[] arr = {10, 20, 30};
int sum = 0;
for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
    sum += arr[i];
}
System.out.println((double) sum / arr.length);
problems.question_raw: What is the average of {10, 20, 30}?

hints.text: Sum = 10+20+30 = 60. Then divide by arr.length = 3.
The cast to double makes this 60.0/3 = 20.0 (not integer division).

problems.question_html:
After running the following code, what is arr[0]?

int[] arr = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
for (int i = 0; i < arr.length / 2; i++) {
    int temp = arr[i];
    arr[i] = arr[arr.length - 1 - i];
    arr[arr.length - 1 - i] = temp;
}
problems.question_raw: After reversing {1,2,3,4,5}, what is arr[0]?

hints.text: This code reverses the array by swapping elements from both ends toward the middle.
After reversal, {1,2,3,4,5} becomes {5,4,3,2,1}. What is arr[0]?

solution_steps.text: The loop swaps arr[0] with arr[4], then arr[1] with arr[3].

problems.question_html:
What is the output?

int[] arr = {3, 1, 4, 1, 5};
int count = 0;
for (int val : arr) {
    if (val > 2) count++;
}
System.out.println(count);
problems.question_raw: How many elements in {3,1,4,1,5} are greater than 2?

hints.text: Count elements where val > 2: check each element against 2.

problems.question_html: What does linearSearch({5, 3, 8, 1}, 6) return?
problems.question_raw: What does linearSearch return when target is not in array?

hints.text: What does the linear search method return when the target is not found?
The loop checks all elements without finding 6, so it returns -1.

solution_steps.text: The method checks each element: 5, 3, 8, 1. None equals 6.
The loop finishes without returning, so it reaches return -1.

problems.question_html:
What is the output?

int[] arr = {2, 4, 6, 8, 10};
for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
    arr[i] = arr[i] * 2;
}
System.out.println(arr[3]);
problems.question_raw: After doubling all elements of {2,4,6,8,10}, what is arr[3]?

hints.text: Each element is doubled. What was arr[3] before doubling?
arr[3] was 8. After doubling: 8 * 2 = 16.

concepts.title: ArrayLists
concepts.content_html:
ArrayList: Dynamic Arrays in Java

An ArrayList is a resizable array implementation from java.util. Unlike arrays, ArrayLists can grow and shrink dynamically.

Creating an ArrayList

import java.util.ArrayList;

ArrayList<String> names = new ArrayList<String>();
ArrayList<Integer> numbers = new ArrayList<>(); // Diamond operator

Note: ArrayLists can only store objects, not primitives. Use wrapper classes: Integer for int, Double for double, etc.

Key Methods

ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add("Alice");       // Add to end
list.add(0, "Bob");      // Insert at index 0
list.get(1);             // Get element at index 1
list.set(0, "Charlie");  // Replace element at index 0
list.remove(0);          // Remove element at index 0
list.size();             // Number of elements

Traversal

// Standard for loop
for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++) {
    System.out.println(list.get(i));
}

// Enhanced for loop
for (String name : list) {
    System.out.println(name);
}

Arrays vs. ArrayLists

FeatureArrayArrayList
SizeFixedDynamic
Syntax for size.length.size()
Accessarr[i]list.get(i)
TypesPrimitives + objectsObjects only
Key Takeaway: Use ArrayLists when you need a collection that can grow or shrink. Use arrays when the size is fixed and you need primitives or maximum performance.
Common Mistake: list.size() uses parentheses (it's a method). arr.length does NOT (it's a field). Mixing these up is a very common AP exam error!

problems.question_html:
What is the output?

ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add("A");
list.add("B");
list.add("C");
System.out.println(list.size());
problems.question_raw: What is the size after adding A, B, C?

hints.text: .size() returns the number of elements in the ArrayList.
Three elements were added: A, B, C.

problems.question_html:
What is the output?

ArrayList<Integer> nums = new ArrayList<>();
nums.add(10);
nums.add(20);
nums.add(30);
nums.remove(1);
System.out.println(nums.get(1));
problems.question_raw: After removing index 1 from [10,20,30], what is get(1)?

hints.text: remove(1) removes the element at index 1 (which is 20). Elements after it shift left.
After removal, the list is [10, 30]. get(1) returns 30.

solution_steps.text: remove(1) removes element at index 1 (value 20). List becomes [10, 30].

problems.question_html:
What is the output?

ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add("X");
list.add("Y");
list.add(0, "Z");
System.out.println(list.get(0));
problems.question_raw: After add(0, 'Z') to [X, Y], what is get(0)?

hints.text: add(0, "Z") inserts "Z" at index 0, shifting everything else right.
List becomes [Z, X, Y]. get(0) returns Z.

solution_steps.text: add(0, "Z") inserts at index 0: [Z, X, Y].

problems.question_html: Which statement correctly replaces the element at index 2 with the value 99?
problems.question_raw: How to replace element at index 2 with 99?

hints.text: add(index, value) inserts; it doesn't replace. What method replaces?
set(index, value) replaces the element at the given index.

solution_steps.text: list.set(2, 99) replaces the element at index 2 with 99.
add(2, 99) would insert, not replace. list[2] uses array syntax, not ArrayList.

problems.question_html:
What is the output?

ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add(5);
list.add(10);
list.add(15);
list.set(1, 99);
for (int n : list) {
    System.out.print(n + " ");
}
problems.question_raw: Output after set(1, 99) on [5, 10, 15]?

hints.text: set(1, 99) replaces the value at index 1 (which was 10) with 99.
List becomes [5, 99, 15]. The loop prints each element followed by a space.

problems.question_html: Why can't you create an ArrayList<int> in Java?
problems.question_raw: Why can't you use ArrayList<int>?

choices.text: int is not a valid data type in Java
ArrayLists can only store objects, not primitives
You need to use int[] instead

hints.text: Java generics (the angle brackets) only work with reference types (objects), not primitive types.
Use Integer (the wrapper class) instead of int.

solution_steps.text: Java generics only accept reference types (objects), not primitives.
int is a primitive type. Integer is its wrapper class.
Use ArrayList<Integer> instead. Java autoboxing handles the conversion automatically.

concepts.title: Common Array/ArrayList Patterns
concepts.content_html:
Important Patterns for the AP Exam

These patterns appear frequently on the AP CS A exam. Understanding them will help you tackle free-response questions with confidence.

Removing Elements During Traversal

When removing elements from an ArrayList during traversal, be careful! If you use a forward loop, you'll skip elements after each removal.

// WRONG: Skips elements after removal
for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++) {
    if (list.get(i) < 0) {
        list.remove(i); // Next element shifts to index i, but i increments!
    }
}

// CORRECT: Traverse backward
for (int i = list.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
    if (list.get(i) < 0) {
        list.remove(i); // Removal doesn't affect indices we haven't visited
    }
}

Shifting Elements

When inserting or removing from the middle of an array, elements must shift:

// Remove element at index k (shift left)
for (int i = k; i < arr.length - 1; i++) {
    arr[i] = arr[i + 1];
}
// Clear the last position
arr[arr.length - 1] = 0;

Parallel Arrays

Sometimes you use two arrays where the same index refers to related data:

String[] names = {"Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"};
int[] scores = {95, 87, 92};
// names[i] corresponds to scores[i]
Key Takeaway: When removing elements from an ArrayList in a loop, traverse backward to avoid skipping elements. This is one of the most commonly tested patterns on the AP exam.
Common Mistake: Using a for-each loop to remove elements causes a ConcurrentModificationException. Always use a standard for loop (preferably backward) when removing.

problems.question_html:
What is the output?

ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add(1); list.add(-2); list.add(3); list.add(-4); list.add(5);
for (int i = list.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
    if (list.get(i) < 0) {
        list.remove(i);
    }
}
System.out.println(list);
problems.question_raw: Remove negatives from [1,-2,3,-4,5] traversing backward. Output?

hints.text: Backward traversal: check i=4 (5, keep), i=3 (-4, remove), i=2 (3, keep), i=1 (-2, remove), i=0 (1, keep).
Both negative values are removed because backward traversal doesn't skip elements.

solution_steps.text: i=2: list.get(2)=3, keep. i=1: list.get(1)=-2, remove. List: [1, 3, 5]. i=0: keep 1.

problems.question_html:
Why does this code fail to remove all negative numbers?

ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add(-1); list.add(-2); list.add(3);
for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++) {
    if (list.get(i) < 0) {
        list.remove(i);
    }
}
// list is [-2, 3] instead of [3]
problems.question_raw: Why does forward removal fail to remove all negatives?

choices.text: The remove method doesn't work on negative numbers
After removing index 0, index 1 shifts to index 0, but i increments to 1, skipping it
The loop should use <= instead of <
You need to use removeAll() instead

hints.text: Think about what happens to the indices after you remove an element.
After removing -1 at index 0, -2 shifts to index 0. But i becomes 1, so -2 is skipped.

solution_steps.text: i increments to 1. list.get(1) = 3. -2 at index 0 was skipped!
This is why backward traversal is the correct approach for removal during iteration.

problems.question_html:
Given parallel arrays, what does this code print?

String[] names = {"Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Diana"};
int[] scores = {85, 92, 78, 96};
int maxIdx = 0;
for (int i = 1; i < scores.length; i++) {
    if (scores[i] > scores[maxIdx]) {
        maxIdx = i;
    }
}
System.out.println(names[maxIdx]);
problems.question_raw: Who has the highest score using parallel arrays?

hints.text: This finds the index of the maximum score, then uses that index to look up the name.
Scores: 85, 92, 78, 96. Max is 96 at index 3. names[3] = ?

problems.question_html:
What is the output?

int[] arr = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
int k = 2;
for (int i = k; i < arr.length - 1; i++) {
    arr[i] = arr[i + 1];
}
arr[arr.length - 1] = 0;
System.out.println(arr[2] + " " + arr[4]);
problems.question_raw: After shifting left from index 2, what are arr[2] and arr[4]?

hints.text: This code removes element at index 2 by shifting elements left.

solution_steps.text: Original: {10, 20, 30, 40, 50}. Removing at k=2 by shifting left.

problems.question_html:
What exception is thrown by this code?

ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add("A"); list.add("B"); list.add("C");
for (String s : list) {
    if (s.equals("B")) {
        list.remove(s);
    }
}
problems.question_raw: What exception from removing in a for-each loop?

hints.text: Modifying an ArrayList while iterating with a for-each loop is not allowed.
Java detects the structural modification and throws a ConcurrentModificationException.

solution_steps.text: The for-each loop uses an Iterator internally.
Calling list.remove() during iteration modifies the list, invalidating the iterator.
Java throws ConcurrentModificationException. Use a standard backward for loop instead.
